title: Dog Sudden Facial Swelling and Hives: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Sudden swelling and hives in dogs are often signs of a severe allergic reaction. Learn when to seek emergency care and why it happens.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden facial swelling and hives in dogs are signs of an acute allergic reaction that requires urgent veterinary attention, as it can progress to life-threatening airway obstruction.

What causes sudden facial swelling and hives in my dog?

When a dog experiences sudden puffiness around the eyes, snout, or ears, along with raised bumps (hives) across the body, it is typically a condition known as angioedema and urticaria. This is an acute allergic reaction. It happens when the immune system overreacts to a specific trigger, causing blood vessels to leak fluid into the skin and deeper tissues.

Is sudden facial swelling in my dog considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Any sudden facial swelling should be treated as a potential emergency.
  • While many cases are limited to skin irritation, swelling can extend to the throat and airway, leading to anaphylaxis.
  • If your dog is wheezing, gasping for breath, vomiting, or acting lethargic, you must transport them to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

How can taking a photo of my dog's swelling help with veterinary triage?

  • Take a quick, clear photo of the swelling and hives before you leave, provided your dog is stable enough to travel.
  • Because allergic reactions evolve rapidly, the swelling may worsen or change by the time you reach the clinic.
  • Showing the veterinarian the initial onset helps them determine the rate of progression and ensures your pet is prioritized correctly during the triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Urticaria (hives or angioedematous plaques) of the skin and subcutaneous tissue and acute edema of the lips, conjunctiva, and skin of the face (facial-conjunctival angioedema) are less severe forms of Type I hypersensitivity. These reactions usually follow administration of vaccines or drugs, ingestion of certain foods, or insect bites. While hives may be the least severe reaction, facial-conjunctival edema is more severe and can be associated with mild to moderately severe systemic anaphylaxis. In severe cases with urticaria, epinephrine should be given intramuscularly, and in cases of anaphylaxis, epinephrine should be administered subcutaneously immediately at a dosage of 1:1,000 (0.1-0.5 mL) for dogs, which can be repeated every 10-20 minutes. When given intravenously, it must be diluted to 1:10,000, and 0.5-1 mL is administered with vigilant monitoring of heart rate, heart rhythm, and blood pressure; intravenous fluids are indicated to prevent vascular collapse. Antihistamines and corticosteroids may also be considered.

Chapter: Emergency, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 824)

faqs.question: What causes sudden hives and swelling in dogs?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include insect stings (bees or wasps), spider bites, reactions to vaccinations, or exposure to specific environmental chemicals and plants.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter antihistamines?
faqs.answer: Do not administer any medication without professional guidance. While vets often use antihistamines, the dosage is weight-specific, and some human formulations contain xylitol, which is toxic to dogs.

faqs.question: How long does an allergic reaction last?
faqs.answer: With veterinary treatment, such as injectable steroids or antihistamines, swelling usually begins to decrease within a few hours, though it may take 24 to 48 hours to resolve completely.
